one_line_takeaway: GraphRAG can be silently subverted by editing a few words of trusted source text -- TKPA achieves ~93% targeted attack success and UKPA halves QA accuracy (95%->50%) with <0.05% of words changed, all while evading current text-level defenses.
key_finding: Modifying only a few words is enough to break GraphRAG. TKPA reaches 93.10% ASR on The Little Prince (91.27% average across three corpora) with QASD 0.83 average, beating PoisonedRAG (70.40% avg ASR) and Naive Swap (16.1% avg). UKPA collapses Microsoft GraphRAG QA accuracy from 95% to 50% (and LightRAG from 90% to 45%), far exceeding the TextFooler-style baseline's drop to 85%, while modifying only 32-60 words (0.033%-0.045%) of very large corpora and severely fragmenting graph topology (edge Jaccard as low as 0.0789 on LightRAG/RUW). Three state-of-the-art defenses fail almost completely: detection F1 near 0 (e.g., 0.13 for LLMDet on TKPA, 0.04 for perplexity filter on UKPA).
core_contribution: The paper identifies and exploits a 'manipulation-only' attack surface in Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG): instead of injecting new malicious documents (the additive strategy of prior work), the adversary makes tiny edits to already-trusted source text (e.g., a few words in a Wikipedia-style corpus) that corrupt the LLM-constructed knowledge graph and thereby mislead downstream reasoning. Two attacks are proposed. (1) Targeted KPA (TKPA) treats poisoning as a network-intervention problem: it uses a graph-theoretic pipeline (Vulnerable Community Localization via a vulnerability score combining degree/betweenness centrality and community-summary length; ego-subgraph extraction; PageRank/semantic/sentiment chunk scoring; then LLM-driven rewriting) to steer specific queries toward attacker-chosen answers. (2) Universal KPA (UKPA) is query-agnostic: it exploits GraphRAG's reliance on coreference/linguistic cues (pronouns, referring expressions) that glue cross-chunk mentions into single entity nodes, subtly rewriting those cues to fragment the global graph, using a surrogate structural-impact score (entity-set diff, relation-set diff, embedding similarity) to pick rewrites without graph access. The paper's own novelty is explicitly the edit-in-place / manipulation-only surface, distinct from the cited GRAGPOISON (Liang et al. 2025), which is additive injection, and PoisonedRAG (used here only as a baseline).
threat_model: Gray-box adversary who can edit a small fraction of the trusted source corpus (e.g., Wikipedia pages) but cannot inject entirely new documents, cannot observe the constructed knowledge graph, and has no access to model parameters. The attacker knows the general GraphRAG pipeline (chunking -> entity/relation extraction -> graph merge -> community detection/summarization -> retrieval). TKPA additionally assumes the attacker understands that answers are driven by communities; UKPA assumes only that a graph is built from text, without structural details. Objectives are precision (TKPA: force specific QA outcomes) and breadth (UKPA: globally degrade reasoning). Stealthiness is implicit, arising from the very small edit budget on otherwise trusted text.
limitations: Evaluation is small-scale: only 3 documents for TKPA and 2 for UKPA, each with 20-30 QA pairs, all long-form narrative/encyclopedic text; no large multi-document corpora or diverse domains. Attack success and QASD are judged by an LLM evaluator (GPT-4o), the same model family used to build the attack, risking circularity. UKPA is tested on two GraphRAG systems but TKPA effectiveness is only reported on Microsoft GraphRAG. Only three older/lightweight defenses are considered (GPT-2 perplexity filter, few-shot LLMDet, spaCy semantic-closeness); no graph-structure-aware or provenance-based defenses are evaluated, despite the authors arguing text-level defenses are the wrong layer. No real-world deployment (e.g., live Wikipedia editing constraints, revert mechanisms) is considered, and there is no proposed defense beyond a call to treat graph construction as a security component.

datasets_used: The Little Prince (LP) narrative text -- 94,496 words (TKPA and UKPA)
Wikipedia: Japanese Asset Price Bubble (JAPB) -- 44,445 words (TKPA)
20-30 GPT-4o-generated, manually reviewed multi-hop QA pairs per document
quantitative_results: TKPA ASR: LP 93.10%, FC08 89.50%, JAPB 91.20%; average 91.27%
TKPA QASD: LP 0.85, FC08 0.81, JAPB 0.83; average 0.83
UKPA downstream QA accuracy: Microsoft GraphRAG 95% -> 50%; LightRAG 90% -> 45%
UKPA graph structural damage (Microsoft GraphRAG / RUW): node retention 0.5648, edge retention 0.2770, node Jaccard 0.4100, edge Jaccard 0.1581; lowest edge Jaccard 0.0789 on LightRAG/RUW
UKPA node/edge counts clean vs attacked (Microsoft GraphRAG/RUW): nodes 347->327, edges 379->390
Modification budget: TKPA LP 48 words min (0.055%), 155 avg (0.164%); UKPA LP 32 words (0.033%), RUW 60 words (0.045%)
Defense detection F1 (near zero): TKPA perplexity-filter 0.07, LLMDet 0.13; UKPA SCC 0.07, perplexity-filter 0.04, LLMDet 0.11
TKPA ablation: equal weights 89.8% ASR, tuned (0.5,0.3,0.2) 91.2%; graph-only 65.3%, semantic-only 58.2%, attitude-only 51.7%; K=1 55.8%, K=2 81.3%, K=3 91.2% (plateaus after)
UKPA ablation: equal weights QA 0.55, tuned (0.25,0.25,0.5) 0.50, single-component 0.70-0.75; small edits (distance <=3) already drop QA 0.95->0.50
baselines_compared: PoisonedRAG / PRAG (Zou et al. 2024) -- injection attack, TKPA baseline: 70.40% avg ASR, 0.67 avg QASD
Naive Swap (NS) -- injects emotional keywords without coherence, TKPA baseline: 16.1% avg ASR, 0.13 avg QASD
TextFooler-style Perturbation / TP (Jin et al. 2020) -- synonym substitution ignoring coreference, UKPA baseline: only drops QA to 85% (vs UKPA's 50%/45%)
Defenses: Perplexity-based Filter (GPT-2), LLM-based Contamination Detector (LLMDet), Semantic Closeness Checking (spaCy)
